was a [Royal Navy Dockyard](/wiki/Royal_Navy_Dockyard "Royal Navy Dockyard") located on the [River Medway](/wiki/River_Medway "River Medway") in [Kent](/wiki/Kent "Kent"). Established in [Chatham](/wiki/Chatham,_Kent "Chatham, Kent") in the mid-16th century, the dockyard subsequently expanded into neighbouring [Gillingham](/wiki/Gillingham,_Kent "Gillingham, Kent"); at its most extensive (in the early 20th century) two-thirds of the dockyard lay in Gillingham, one-third in Chatham.

This article is about the former Royal Navy dockyard in Chatham, Kent. For the maritime heritage site, see [Chatham Historic Dockyard](/wiki/Chatham_Historic_Dockyard "Chatham Historic Dockyard").

**Chatham Dockyard** was a [Royal Navy Dockyard](/wiki/Royal_Navy_Dockyard "Royal Navy Dockyard") located on the [River Medway](/wiki/River_Medway "River Medway") in [Kent](/wiki/Kent "Kent"). Established in [Chatham](/wiki/Chatham,_Kent "Chatham, Kent") in the mid-16th century, the dockyard subsequently expanded into neighbouring [Gillingham](/wiki/Gillingham,_Kent "Gillingham, Kent"); at its most extensive (in the early 20th century) two-thirds of the dockyard lay in Gillingham, one-third in Chatham.

It came into existence at the time when, following the [Reformation](/wiki/English_Reformation "English Reformation"), relations with the [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic "Catholic") countries of [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe") had worsened, leading to a requirement for additional defences. Over 414 years Chatham Royal Dockyard provided more than 500 ships for the [Royal Navy](/wiki/Royal_Navy "Royal Navy"), and was at the forefront of [shipbuilding](/wiki/Shipbuilding "Shipbuilding"), [industrial](/wiki/Industrial_technology "Industrial technology") and [architectural](/wiki/British_industrial_architecture "British industrial architecture") technology. At its height, it employed over 10,000 skilled [artisans](/wiki/Artisan "Artisan") and covered 400 acres (1.6 km2). Chatham dockyard closed in 1984, and 84 acres (34 ha) of the Georgian dockyard is now managed as the [Chatham Historic Dockyard](/wiki/Chatham_Historic_Dockyard "Chatham Historic Dockyard") visitor attraction by the Chatham Historic Dockyard Trust.

## Overview

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chatham_Dockyard&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Overview")]

[Joseph Farington](/wiki/Joseph_Farington "Joseph Farington") (1747–1821) was commissioned by the [Navy Board](/wiki/Navy_Board "Navy Board") to paint a panoramic view of Chatham Dockyard (as part of a project to create a visual record of all six home yards) in 1785.[[1]](#cite_note-RMG_Farington-1) The painting, now in the [National Maritime Museum](/wiki/National_Maritime_Museum "National Maritime Museum"), provides a detailed illustration of the yard as it was in the [Age of Sail](/wiki/Age_of_Sail "Age of Sail"); many of the buildings and structures illustrated survive:

[![Painting of the Dockyard](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/14/Joseph_Farington_%281747-1821%29_-_Chatham_Dockyard_-_BHC1782_-_Royal_Museums_Greenwich.jpg/960px-Joseph_Farington_%281747-1821%29_-_Chatham_Dockyard_-_BHC1782_-_Royal_Museums_Greenwich.jpg)](/wiki/File:Joseph_Farington_(1747-1821)_-_Chatham_Dockyard_-_BHC1782_-_Royal_Museums_Greenwich.jpg "Looking from right to left (i.e. south to north) along the river bank can be seen: the two Anchor Wharf Storehouses (with the Rope House and associated buildings behind); two shipbuilding slips (between which can be seen the Commissioner's House with its large garden, beyond which is the Sail and Colour Loft); two dry docks (with Clock Tower Storehouse behind them, and the Officers' Terrace beyond); the old Smithery (later demolished); two more dry docks (beyond which can be seen the Masthouses and Mouldloft); further building slips (with the two Mast Ponds beyond them); and some Boat Houses (later demolished). In the distance (far left) St Mary's Island can be seen, and ships at anchor on Gillingham Reach. In the centre of the painting, beyond the walls of the Dockyard, is the town of Brompton and, to the right, Chatham Barracks.")

Looking from right to left (i.e. south to north) along the river bank can be seen: the two Anchor Wharf Storehouses (with the Rope House and associated buildings behind); two shipbuilding slips (between which can be seen the Commissioner's House with its large garden, beyond which is the Sail and Colour Loft); two dry docks (with Clock Tower Storehouse behind them, and the Officers' Terrace beyond); the old Smithery (later demolished); two more dry docks (beyond which can be seen the Masthouses and Mouldloft); further building slips (with the two Mast Ponds beyond them); and some Boat Houses (later demolished). In the distance (far left) St Mary's Island can be seen, and ships at anchor on Gillingham Reach. In the centre of the painting, beyond the walls of the Dockyard, is the town of Brompton and, to the right, Chatham Barracks.

### Descriptions of the working dockyard

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chatham_Dockyard&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Descriptions of the working dockyard")]

[William Camden](/wiki/William_Camden "William Camden") (1551–1623) described Chatham dockyard as

> ...stored for the finest fleet the sun ever beheld, and ready at a minute’s warning, built lately by our most gracious sovereign Elizabeth at great expense for the security of her subjects and the terror of her enemies, with a fort on the shore for its defence.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

[![Late 18th-century illustration of the dockyard](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/92/The_Royal_Dockyard_at_Chatham%2C_1789.jpg/330px-The_Royal_Dockyard_at_Chatham%2C_1789.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Royal_Dockyard_at_Chatham,_1789.jpg)

The Dockyard as depicted by [Robert Dodd](/wiki/Robert_Dodd_(artist) "Robert Dodd (artist)") in 1789

[Daniel Defoe](/wiki/Daniel_Defoe "Daniel Defoe") (c. 1660 – 1731), visiting the yard in 1705, also spoke of its achievements with an almost incredulous enthusiasm:

> So great is the order and application there, that a first-rate vessel of war of 106 guns, ordered to be commissioned by Sir [Cloudesley Shovell](/wiki/Cloudesley_Shovell "Cloudesley Shovell"), was ready in three days. At the time the order was given the vessel was entirely unrigged; yet the masts were raised, sails bent, anchors and cables on board, in that time.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

[Charles Dickens](/wiki/Charles_Dickens "Charles Dickens") (1812–1870), who had grown up in Chatham, returned in later life and described in 1861 the novel sight (and sounds) of a ship being built for the first time of iron (rather than wood):

> Twelve hundred men are working at her now; twelve hundred men working on stages over her sides, over her bows, over her sterns, under her keel, between her decks, down in her hold, within her and without, crawling and creeping into the finest curves of her lines wherever it is possible for men to twist. Twelve hundred hammerers, measurers, caulkers, armourers, forgers, smiths, shipwrights; twelve hundred dingers, clashers, dongers, rattlers, clinkers, bangers, bangers, bangers![[4]](#cite_note-4)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chatham_Dockyard&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d7/ChathamMedway.jpg/250px-ChathamMedway.jpg)](/wiki/File:ChathamMedway.jpg)

Engraving of "Chatham Dockyard from [Fort Pitt](/wiki/Fort_Pitt,_Kent "Fort Pitt, Kent")" from Ireland's History of Kent, Vol. 4, 1831. Facing p. 349. Drawn by G. Sheppard, engraved by R. Roffe.

### Gillingham Water

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chatham_Dockyard&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Gillingham Water")]

Chatham's establishment as a naval dockyard was precipitated by the use of the Medway as a safe [anchorage](/wiki/Anchorage_(shipping) "Anchorage (shipping)") by the ships of what became (under [King Henry VIII](/wiki/Henry_VIII_of_England "Henry VIII of England")) England's permanent [Royal Navy](/wiki/Royal_Navy "Royal Navy"). In 1550, a decree was issued to the [Lord High Admiral](/wiki/Lord_High_Admiral_of_England "Lord High Admiral of England") that:

> all the Kinges shippes should be harborowed in [Jillyngham](/wiki/Gillingham,_Medway "Gillingham, Medway") Water – saving only those that be at [Portsmouth](/wiki/Portsmouth "Portsmouth")[[5]](#cite_note-5)

Even prior to this, there is evidence of certain shore facilities being established in the vicinity for the benefit of the King's ships at anchor: there are isolated references from as early as 1509 to the hiring of a [storehouse](/wiki/Warehouse "Warehouse") nearby[[6]](#cite_note-20th_century:_Intro-6) and from 1547 this becomes a fixed item in the Treasurer's annual accounts. (At around the same time a [victualling](/wiki/Victualling_Commissioners "Victualling Commissioners") store was also established, in nearby [Rochester](/wiki/Rochester,_Kent "Rochester, Kent"), to provide the ships and their crews with food.) The storehouse would have furnished ships with such necessary consumables as rope, pulleys, sailcloth and timber. [Careening](/wiki/Careening "Careening") took place on the river, according to a Privy Council instruction of 1550;[[7]](#cite_note-7) for more specialised repairs and maintenance, however, ships would have had to travel to one of the purpose-built [royal dockyards](/wiki/Royal_dockyard "Royal dockyard") (the nearest being those on the [Thames](/wiki/Thames "Thames"): [Deptford](/wiki/Deptford_Dockyard "Deptford Dockyard") and [Woolwich](/wiki/Woolwich_Dockyard "Woolwich Dockyard")).[[8]](#cite_note-SL48-8)

### The early dockyard

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chatham_Dockyard&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: The early dockyard")]

1567 is generally seen as the date of Chatham's establishment as a Royal Naval Dockyard.[[9]](#cite_note-9) In the years that followed the ground was prepared, accommodation was secured and in 1570 a mast pond was installed. The following year a [forge](/wiki/Forge "Forge") was built for anchor-making. At around the same time a large house was leased (the Hill House) for administrative purposes including meetings of the [Council of Marine Causes](/wiki/Council_of_the_Marine "Council of the Marine"). (Hill House would serve as the dockyard's Pay Office for the next 180 years; the [Royal Marine Barracks](/wiki/Royal_Marine_Barracks,_Chatham "Royal Marine Barracks, Chatham") were later built on its site).[[10]](#cite_note-TheHillHouse-10)

The renowned Tudor shipwright [Mathew Baker](/wiki/Mathew_Baker_(shipwright) "Mathew Baker (shipwright)") was appointed to Chatham in 1572 (though he was primarily based at Deptford). Under his supervision the site was developed to include [sawpits](/wiki/Sawpit "Sawpit"), [workshops](/wiki/Workshops "Workshops"), storehouses and a [wharf](/wiki/Wharf "Wharf") with a [treadmill crane](/wiki/Treadmill_crane "Treadmill crane") (completed in 1580). Most significantly, Chatham's first [dry dock](/wiki/Dry_dock "Dry dock") was opened in 1581 (for repairing naval [galleys](/wiki/Galleys "Galleys")).[[11]](#cite_note-11) The first ship to be built at the dockyard, a 10-gun [pinnace](/wiki/Full-rigged_pinnace "Full-rigged pinnace") named HMS *Merlin* (or *Merlyon*), was launched in 1579.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

The dockyard received its first royal visit, from [Elizabeth I](/wiki/Elizabeth_I_of_England "Elizabeth I of England"), in 1573; later, in 1606, [James I](/wiki/James_VI_and_I "James VI and I") used Chatham dockyard for a meeting with [Christian IV of Denmark](/wiki/Christian_IV_of_Denmark "Christian IV of Denmark").[[13]](#cite_note-13)

### Relocation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chatham_Dockyard&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Relocation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/84/Ships_Laid_Up_in_the_Medway_RMG_BHC0832.tiff/lossy-page1-250px-Ships_Laid_Up_in_the_Medway_RMG_BHC0832.tiff.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ships_Laid_Up_in_the_Medway_RMG_BHC0832.tiff)

17th-century painting of naval vessels moored on the River Medway, viewed from Chatham with [Rochester Bridge](/wiki/Rochester_Bridge "Rochester Bridge") in the background.

In the early 17th century the government resolved to invest in a new specialised facility for refitting and repairing warships. By 1611 Chatham had been chosen as its location (in preference to Deptford, which at the time was the nation's principal naval shipbuilding yard; this led to speculation that Deptford was going to be sold off). The decision established Chatham as the country's premier naval industrial complex; nevertheless, concerns were already being raised over its river being prone to silting.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

The decision required the dockyard to move from its original location, which was too constricted, to a new (adjacent) site to the north.[[15]](#cite_note-guide28-15) (The old site was in due course transferred to the [Ordnance Board](/wiki/Ordnance_Board "Ordnance Board"), who established the [gun wharf](#The_Gun_Wharf) there.) By 1619, the new dockyard consisted of a new dry dock and wharf with storehouses, all enclosed within a brick perimeter wall. The growing importance of the dockyard was illustrated with the addition soon afterwards of a mast pond, and the granting of additional land on which a second (double) dry dock was constructed, along with a [sail loft](/wiki/Sail_loft "Sail loft"), a [ropery](/wiki/Ropery "Ropery") and residences for the dockyard officers: all of which were completed by 1624.[[16]](#cite_note-guide15-16)

[Peter Pett](/wiki/Peter_Pett_(shipwright,_died_1672) "Peter Pett (shipwright, died 1672)"), of the [family of shipwrights](/wiki/The_Pett_Dynasty "The Pett Dynasty") whose history is closely connected to the Chatham dockyard, became commissioner in 1649.[[17]](#cite_note-guide29-17) In 1686 two new dry docks were built, in addition to the old single and double dry docks; all four were rebuilt and expanded at various points in subsequent centuries (the double dock having been converted into a single dock in 1703). Although the yard focused mainly on refitting and repairs, some shipbuilding continued to take place. It made do with a single shipbuilding [slip](/wiki/Slipway "Slipway") for much of the 17th century (a second slip, dating from the same period, had fallen out of use; it was replaced in the 1730s). Also in 1686 a 'Great Long Store-house' was built, alongside the ropery on what is now Anchor Wharf;[[10]](#cite_note-TheHillHouse-10) and two new mast ponds were constructed, in what was then the northernmost part of the yard, in 1697 and 1702.[[18]](#cite_note-guide27-18)

#### Sheerness

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chatham_Dockyard&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Sheerness")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/49/Dutch_Attack_on_the_Medway%2C_June_1667_van_Soest_RMG_BHC0295.jpg/250px-Dutch_Attack_on_the_Medway%2C_June_1667_van_Soest_RMG_BHC0295.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dutch_Attack_on_the_Medway,_June_1667_van_Soest_RMG_BHC0295.jpg)

*Dutch [Attack on the Medway](/wiki/Raid_on_the_Medway "Raid on the Medway"), June 1667* by [Pieter Cornelisz van Soest](/wiki/Pieter_Cornelisz_van_Soest "Pieter Cornelisz van Soest"), painted c. 1667. The captured ship [*Royal Charles*](/wiki/HMS_Royal_Charles_(1660) "HMS Royal Charles (1660)") is right of centre.

One of the disadvantages of Chatham (and also of the Thames-side yards) was their relative inaccessibility for ships at sea (including those anchored in [The Nore](/wiki/The_Nore "The Nore")). Therefore, rather than risk being constrained by wind, tide and [draught](/wiki/Draught_(hull) "Draught (hull)") on a journey upriver, ships would seek as often as possible to do running repairs and maintenance while at anchor, and would only travel to the dockyard when necessary. Thus deliveries of victuals, ordnance and other supplies were made by small boats, sailing regularly between Chatham and The Nore.[[19]](#cite_note-Hughes2002-19)

Seeking to alleviate this less-than-satisfactory situation, the [Navy Board](/wiki/Navy_Board "Navy Board") explored options for developing a shore facility with direct access from the open water of the [Thames Estuary](/wiki/Thames_Estuary "Thames Estuary"). The escalating [Anglo-Dutch wars](/wiki/Anglo-Dutch_wars "Anglo-Dutch wars") forced their hand, however: several temporary buildings were hastily erected in [Sheerness](/wiki/Sheerness "Sheerness"), at the mouth of the Medway, to enable ships to re-arm, re-victual and (if necessary) be repaired as quickly as possible. In 1665, the Navy Board approved Sheerness as a site for a new dockyard, and building work began; but in 1667 the still-incomplete [Sheerness Dockyard](/wiki/Sheerness_Dockyard "Sheerness Dockyard") was captured by the [Dutch Navy](/wiki/Dutch_Navy "Dutch Navy") and used as the base for an [attack](/wiki/Raid_on_the_Medway "Raid on the Medway") on the English fleet at anchor in the Medway itself. Sheerness remained operational as a royal dockyard until 1959, but it was never considered a major shore establishment and in several respects it operated as a subsidiary yard to Chatham.[[20]](#cite_note-Coad2013-20)

### Consolidation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chatham_Dockyard&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Consolidation")]

By the late 17th century a number of prestigious new buildings were erected (including the officers' residences, the clocktower storehouse and the main gatehouse), several of which are still in place. At the same time, the nearby village of [Brompton](/wiki/Brompton,_Kent "Brompton, Kent") began to be developed to provide housing for the dockyard's growing workforce.[[10]](#cite_note-TheHillHouse-10)

From the very start of the 18th century, however, Chatham began to be superseded in both size and importance, first by [Portsmouth](/wiki/HMNB_Portsmouth "HMNB Portsmouth"), then [Plymouth](/wiki/HMNB_Devonport "HMNB Devonport"), when the main naval enemy became France, and the Western approaches the chief theatre of operations. In addition, the Medway had begun to silt up, making navigation more difficult (especially as the Navy's ships were getting larger). As a result, it was acknowledged by 1771 that Chatham had no future as a front-line fleet base;[[20]](#cite_note-Coad2013-20) nevertheless, following a visit by the [Admiralty Board](/wiki/Admiralty_Board_(United_Kingdom) "Admiralty Board (United Kingdom)") in 1773, the decision was taken to invest further in Chatham, and to develop it as a building yard rather than a refitting base.[[17]](#cite_note-guide29-17)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ab/A_Plan_of_His_Majesty%27s_Naval_Yard_at_Chatham%2C_1774.jpg/960px-A_Plan_of_His_Majesty%27s_Naval_Yard_at_Chatham%2C_1774.jpg)](/wiki/File:A_Plan_of_His_Majesty%27s_Naval_Yard_at_Chatham,_1774.jpg "A detailed plan of the Dockyard, published in 1774.")

A detailed plan of the Dockyard, published in 1774.

By this time the establishment, including the gun wharf, stretched one mile (1.6 km) in length, and included an area of in excess of 95 acres (380,000 m2). Alongside the four dry docks it now had a total of six shipbuilding slips (equalling Deptford and outnumbering the other yards in this regard), albeit three of the six were under 150 ft (46 m) in length and suitable only for building smaller warships.[[20]](#cite_note-Coad2013-20) The docks varied from 160 ft (49 m) to 186 ft (57 m) in length. The officers and men employed in the yard had also increased, and by 1798 they numbered 1,664, including 49 officers and clerks and 624 [shipwrights](/wiki/Shipwright "Shipwright"). Additionally required were the blockmakers, caulkers, pitch-heaters, [blacksmiths](/wiki/Blacksmith "Blacksmith"), [joiners](/wiki/Joiner "Joiner") and [carpenters](/wiki/Carpenter "Carpenter"), sail makers, [riggers](/wiki/Rigging "Rigging"), and [ropemakers](/wiki/Ropemaker "Ropemaker") (274), as well as [bricklayers](/wiki/Bricklayer "Bricklayer"), labourers and others.[[21]](#cite_note-21) Building works at Chatham did not compare with the substantial expansions underway at Portsmouth and Plymouth at this time; but the southern part of the yard was significantly redeveloped, with construction of two new storehouses on Anchor Wharf and a major reconfiguration of the ropery.[[20]](#cite_note-Coad2013-20)

Among the vessels built in this Dockyard which still exist are [HMS *Victory*](/wiki/HMS_Victory "HMS Victory") (launched in 1765 and now preserved at [Portsmouth Historic Dockyard](/wiki/Portsmouth_Historic_Dockyard "Portsmouth Historic Dockyard"))[[22]](#cite_note-22) and [HMS *Unicorn*](/wiki/HMS_Unicorn_(1824) "HMS Unicorn (1824)") (a [*Leda*-class frigate](/wiki/Leda-class_frigate "Leda-class frigate")), launched in 1824 and now preserved afloat at [Dundee](/wiki/Dundee "Dundee")).[[23]](#cite_note-23)

### Mechanisation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chatham_Dockyard&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Mechanisation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/59/EW%281884%29_p.039_-_Chatham_-_A_%2B_C_Black_%28pub%29.jpg/250px-EW%281884%29_p.039_-_Chatham_-_A_%2B_C_Black_%28pub%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:EW(1884)_p.039_-_Chatham_-_A_%2B_C_Black_(pub).jpg)

1884 map, showing the 'Royal Dock Yard' (centre) with the river to the west, new extension to the north, barracks and fortifications to the east.

Following the appointment of [Robert Seppings](/wiki/Robert_Seppings "Robert Seppings") as Master Shipwright in 1804, iron began to be introduced into the structure of ships being built at Chatham; the following year work began on a new, much larger [smithery](/wiki/Smithery "Smithery"), commissioned by [Samuel Bentham](/wiki/Samuel_Bentham "Samuel Bentham"), designed by [Edward Holl](/wiki/Edward_Holl "Edward Holl") and fitted out by [John Rennie](/wiki/John_Rennie_the_Elder "John Rennie the Elder").[[24]](#cite_note-SmitheryListing-24) Among other things, the use of iron in ship construction enabled larger vessels to be built, and between 1836 and 1851 Chatham gained five new covered slipways, much larger in scale than the ones they replaced. (The covering of Chatham's slipways and dry docks, to protect the woodwork of ships as they were built or repaired, had begun with No.2 Slip and No.1 Dock in 1817).[[25]](#cite_note-no.1dock-25)

In 1811, [Marc Brunel](/wiki/Marc_Brunel "Marc Brunel") recommended the installation of steam-powered sawmills in the royal dockyards, to replace the manual labour of the [saw pits](/wiki/Saw_pit "Saw pit"). Money was only made available for one such installation, however, and Chatham (as the principal building yard at this time) was chosen as its location. Land was purchased to the northeast for its construction, and the new saw mill began operation in 1814.[[20]](#cite_note-Coad2013-20) The following year, John Rennie was engaged to build an entirely new dry dock (following his own recommendations) which was the first in the dockyard to be built entirely of stone; it was built on the site of the old smithery. Unlike the earlier timber docks, which were drained using gravity, this new dock (No.3 Dock) was pumped dry using a [Boulton & Watt](/wiki/Boulton_%26_Watt "Boulton & Watt") steam engine. After completion of the new dock in 1821, reconstruction of the other docks in stone followed (with the exception of the northernmost, which was converted into a slipway); they were likewise emptied using steam power, provided by the same engine and pumps (which were also linked to a network of pipes installed across the whole dockyard site for firefighting purposes).[[20]](#cite_note-Coad2013-20) Another novel application of steam power was embarked on in 1817, with the building of a 'Lead and Paint Mill', in which a single beam engine powered a [rolling mill](/wiki/Rolling_mill "Rolling mill") and a series of devices for grinding pigment and mixing paint; the plant was operational from 1819. It was not until 1837, however, that steam power was first introduced into the ropery, and the smithery received its first engine (for blowing the forges and powering [tilt hammers](/wiki/Tilt_hammer "Tilt hammer")) in 1841.[[20]](#cite_note-Coad2013-20)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/HMS_Royal_Oak_%281862%29.jpg/250px-HMS_Royal_Oak_%281862%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:HMS_Royal_Oak_(1862).jpg)

Armour plating being fitted to [HMS *Royal Oak*](/wiki/HMS_Royal_Oak_(1862) "HMS Royal Oak (1862)") at Chatham, c. 1862.

At the same time, moves were being made towards the application of steam power to ship propulsion. The first steam-powered ship to be laid down at Chatham was [HMS *Phoenix*](/wiki/HMS_Phoenix_(1832) "HMS Phoenix (1832)"), one of four [paddle steamers](/wiki/Paddle_steamers "Paddle steamers") built concurrently across the royal dockyards in the early 1830s, each designed by a different leading shipwright. [HMS *Bee*](/w/index.php?title=HMS_Bee_(1842)&action=edit&redlink=1 "HMS Bee (1842) (page does not exist)"), launched at Chatham in 1842, was an experimental vessel fitted with both paddles and a [propeller](/wiki/Propeller "Propeller"), each of which could be driven independently from the same engine for comparison. Following the success of such early trials with [screw propulsion](/wiki/Screw-propeller_steamer "Screw-propeller steamer"), several older sailing ships were taken into dry dock and [retro-fitted](/wiki/Retrofitting "Retrofitting") with propellers, beginning with [HMS *Horatio*](/wiki/HMS_Horatio_(1807) "HMS Horatio (1807)").[[26]](#cite_note-26) Another hint of changes to come was seen in the launch of [HMS *Aetna*](/wiki/HMS_Aetna_(1855) "HMS Aetna (1855)"), the first [armour-plated](/wiki/Ironclad_warship "Ironclad warship") vessel to be built at Chatham, in 1856. All these developments were to come together with the construction of [HMS *Achilles*](/wiki/HMS_Achilles_(1863) "HMS Achilles (1863)"), in a newly expanded No.2 Dock, between 1860 and 1864: the first true iron-hulled battleship to be launched in a royal dockyard.[[27]](#cite_note-passage-27)

To meet the new demands of building in iron, metal mills were built alongside the smithery in 1845, containing [rolling](/wiki/Rolling_(metalworking) "Rolling (metalworking)") machinery and [furnaces](/wiki/Metallurgical_furnace "Metallurgical furnace") for reprocessing iron.[[20]](#cite_note-Coad2013-20) Holl's smithery was itself enlarged with the addition of a [foundry](/wiki/Foundry "Foundry") in the 1850s, and its courtyard was roofed over for a [steam hammer](/wiki/Steam_hammer "Steam hammer") shop in 1865.[[24]](#cite_note-SmitheryListing-24) By 1861, No.1 Dock had been filled in and a machine shop constructed in its place for heating, bending and planing armour plate for HMS *Achilles* which was being built alongside.[[25]](#cite_note-no.1dock-25) For the time being, however, [marine steam engines](/wiki/Marine_steam_engine "Marine steam engine") were not manufactured on site but were ordered from [Thames](/wiki/Thames "Thames")-based private contractors (along with other associated equipment).[[28]](#cite_note-28) In 1860 the dockyard's policing was also transferred to the new [No.4 Division of the Metropolitan Police](/wiki/History_of_the_Ministry_of_Defence_Police#1860:_The_Metropolitan_Police "History of the Ministry of Defence Police"), which remained in that role until 1932.[[29]](#cite_note-29)

### Expansion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chatham_Dockyard&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Expansion")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8d/HMS_Empress_of_India_Stern_Drydock.jpg/250px-HMS_Empress_of_India_Stern_Drydock.jpg)](/wiki/File:HMS_Empress_of_India_Stern_Drydock.jpg)

[HMS *Empress of India*](/wiki/HMS_Empress_of_India "HMS Empress of India") in No 8 Dock, with No 1 Boiler Shop in the background, c. 1897.

A significant disadvantage for Chatham was that [fitting out](/wiki/Fitting_out "Fitting out") had always taken place on the river. When *Achilles*, for example, had been completed and floated out of dry dock, she spent almost a year moored in Gillingham Reach, where not only her engines, boilers, funnels and a 2.5-long-ton (2.54 t) propeller were fitted, but also masts, sails, rigging, guns, coal, food, ammunition and furnishings were either loaded or installed.[[27]](#cite_note-passage-27) It was partly to address this problem that the Admiralty undertook a huge building programme at Chatham. Between 1862 and 1865, the size of the yard quadrupled and provided specialist facilities for steam-powered ships with metal hulls. Three basins were constructed along St. Mary's creek, from west to east: No.1 Basin (of 28 acres (110,000 m2)), No.2 Basin (20 acres (81,000 m2)) and No.3 Basin (21 acres (85,000 m2)). Along the southern edge of No.1 Basin four new dry docks were built (Nos.5–8), each 420 ft (130 m) long. Initially a purpose-built 'steam factory' was planned, but following the closure of Woolwich Dockyard in 1869 a number of slip covers were removed from that site and re-erected at the head of the new dry docks to serve as factories for building and fitting engines and for [boilermaking](/wiki/Boilermaking "Boilermaking").[[20]](#cite_note-Coad2013-20)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/54/Machinery_at_Chatham_Dockyard_2.jpg/250px-Machinery_at_Chatham_Dockyard_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Machinery_at_Chatham_Dockyard_2.jpg)

Plate-bending roller, installed in No 5 Machine Shop in 1913 and now preserved at the Historic Dockyard.

No.1 Basin was officially opened in 1871, with [HMS *Invincible*](/wiki/HMS_Invincible_(1869) "HMS Invincible (1869)") being brought into No.5 Dock for repairs, with great ceremony.[[30]](#cite_note-30) Work on the other docks and basins followed, with No.3 Basin finally being completed in 1883. Two years later the project was largely complete, with facilities provided alongside for gun mounting and mast rigging, as well as a [victualling](/wiki/Victualling "Victualling") depot and a [coaling](/wiki/Coaling_(ships) "Coaling (ships)") area.[[20]](#cite_note-Coad2013-20) Much of the excavation and building work had been done by [convict labour](/wiki/Convict_labour "Convict labour") (the [Chatham Convict Prison](/wiki/Chatham_Convict_Prison "Chatham Convict Prison") having been built to the north of the dockyard, with the expansion project in mind, in 1853).[[31]](#cite_note-31) In 1897 a [naval barracks](/wiki/Royal_Naval_Barracks,_Chatham "Royal Naval Barracks, Chatham") was built on the site of the prison to provide crew accommodation for ships anchored in [The Nore](/wiki/The_Nore "The Nore"); for the next sixty years it served as the headquarters of Nore Command, whose [Commander-in-Chief](/wiki/Commander-in-Chief,_The_Nore "Commander-in-Chief, The Nore") was accommodated in the adjacent Admiralty House.[[20]](#cite_note-Coad2013-20)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/20/The_dockyard_Chatham_England.jpg/250px-The_dockyard_Chatham_England.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_dockyard_Chatham_England.jpg)

The Dockyard extension viewed from [Upnor](/wiki/Upnor "Upnor"), c.1910.

In 1897 a new, even longer dry dock was opened on the north side of No.1 Basin: at its opening, this (No.9 Dock) was the largest in the world at 650 ft (200 m) long by 84 ft (26 m) wide. At around the same time, in the older part of the dockyard, No.7 Slip was extended to accommodate the building of [HMS *Prince of Wales*](/wiki/HMS_Prince_of_Wales_(1902) "HMS Prince of Wales (1902)") (launched in 1902), and a new (uncovered) slipway was built a little to the north (No.8 Slip, completed in 1900); at 616 ft (188 m) this was one of the longest slips in the world and was designed for building [battleships](/wiki/Battleships "Battleships").[[32]](#cite_note-32) (The older slipways, by contrast, were proving much too small and they were mostly filled in around this time, their covered areas being put to alternative uses.) The first battleship to be built on the new No.8 Slip was [HMS *Africa*](/wiki/HMS_Africa_(1905) "HMS Africa (1905)"), launched in 1905; however it also proved to be the last, as it was announced (controversially) that Chatham Dockyard would be unable to accommodate [Dreadnoughts](/wiki/Dreadnoughts "Dreadnoughts"). Proposals were made for a fourth Basin of 57 acres (230,000 m2), together with additional large docks of up to 800 ft (240 m), to cover the remaining land on St Mary's Island; but these were soon superseded by plans to build an entirely new dockyard at [Rosyth](/wiki/Rosyth_Dockyard "Rosyth Dockyard").[[33]](#cite_note-33)

Nevertheless, [Light cruisers](/wiki/Light_cruisers "Light cruisers") and other smaller vessels continued to be built at Chatham during the first half of the 20th century.[[34]](#cite_note-34) Also with the 20th century came the submarine. The first submarine to be built at a royal dockyard was [HMS *C17*](/wiki/HMS_C17 "HMS C17"), launched from the covered No.7 Slip in 1908 and then fitted out in No.2 Dock; five more of the same [class](/wiki/British_C-class_submarine "British C-class submarine") followed, [C18](/wiki/HMS_C18 "HMS C18"), [C19](/wiki/HMS_C19 "HMS C19"), [C20](/wiki/HMS_C20 "HMS C20"), [C33](/wiki/HMS_C33 "HMS C33") and [C34](/wiki/HMS_C34 "HMS C34").[[35]](#cite_note-Boniface-35) During [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), twelve submarines were built here, but when hostilities ceased, uncompleted boats were scrapped and five years passed before a further ship was launched. In the interwar years, eight [S-class submarines](/wiki/British_S-class_submarine_(1931) "British S-class submarine (1931)") as well as X1 were built at Chatham but this was a period of decline.[[36]](#cite_note-36)
Production ramped up during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") with HMS's *[Umpire](/wiki/HMS_Umpire_(N82) "HMS Umpire (N82)")*, *[Una](/wiki/HMS_Una "HMS Una")*, *[Splendid](/wiki/HMS_Splendid_(P228) "HMS Splendid (P228)")*, *[Sportsman](/wiki/HMS_Sportsman "HMS Sportsman")*, *[Shalimar](/wiki/HMS_Shalimar "HMS Shalimar")*, *[Tradewind](/wiki/HMS_Tradewind_(P329) "HMS Tradewind (P329)")*, *[Trenchant](/wiki/HMS_Trenchant_(P331) "HMS Trenchant (P331)")*, *[Turpin](/wiki/HMS_Turpin_(P354) "HMS Turpin (P354)")*, *[Thermopylae](/wiki/HMS_Thermopylae_(P355) "HMS Thermopylae (P355)")* and *[Acheron](/wiki/HMS_Acheron_(P411) "HMS Acheron (P411)")* being constructed.[[35]](#cite_note-Boniface-35)

### Last years

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chatham_Dockyard&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Last years")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/24/Chatham_Naval_Dockyard_%2816433123107%29.jpg/250px-Chatham_Naval_Dockyard_%2816433123107%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Chatham_Naval_Dockyard_(16433123107).jpg)

[Navy Day](/wiki/Navy_Day#United_Kingdom "Navy Day") at HMNB Chatham, c.1977

In February 1958 it was announced in Parliament that Sheerness Dockyard would close in 1960, with [Nore Command](/wiki/Nore_Command "Nore Command") (and its Chatham-based Commander-in-Chief) to be abolished the following year. At the same time, it was made clear that at Chatham "the dockyard will be retained; but the barracks and other naval establishments will be closed".[[37]](#cite_note-37) (In the event, the barracks were reprieved and repurposed rather than being closed at this stage.)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7c/Number_3_Dry_Dock._Chatham.jpg/250px-Number_3_Dry_Dock._Chatham.jpg)](/wiki/File:Number_3_Dry_Dock._Chatham.jpg)

Rennie's No 3 Dock of 1816–21; today it contains HMS *Ocelot*, the last Royal Navy vessel built at Chatham.

The final boats constructed in Chatham were [*Oberon*-class submarines](/wiki/Oberon-class_submarine "Oberon-class submarine") – [*Ocelot*](/wiki/HMS_Ocelot "HMS Ocelot") was the last vessel built for the [Royal Navy](/wiki/Royal_Navy "Royal Navy"), and the final vessel was [*Okanagan*](/wiki/HMCS_Okanagan "HMCS Okanagan") built for the [Royal Canadian Navy](/wiki/Royal_Canadian_Navy "Royal Canadian Navy") and launched on 17 September 1966.[[35]](#cite_note-Boniface-35) In 1968, a [nuclear](/wiki/Nuclear_reactor "Nuclear reactor") submarine refitting complex was built between Nos 6 and 7 dry docks, complete with refuelling cranes and health physics building. In spite of this in June 1981, it was announced to Parliament that the dockyard would be run down and closed in 1984.[[38]](#cite_note-38)

#### Closure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chatham_Dockyard&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Closure")]

The closure of Chatham Dockyard (along with the adjacent Naval Barracks) was announced in Parliament in June 1981 and scheduled to take place in 1984.[[39]](#cite_note-39) [Redundancy notices](/wiki/Redundancy_in_United_Kingdom_law "Redundancy in United Kingdom law") were served, but then abruptly withdrawn following the [1982 invasion of the Falkland Islands](/wiki/1982_invasion_of_the_Falkland_Islands "1982 invasion of the Falkland Islands"); the dockyard was heavily involved in preparing ships for the South Atlantic, and in repairing damaged vessels on their return. Nonetheless, the dockyard closed, as planned, on 31 March 1984.[[40]](#cite_note-40)

### Regeneration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chatham_Dockyard&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Regeneration")]

At the time of its closure the dockyard covered 400 acres (1.6 km2). Thereafter this was divided into three sections:

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/61/Anchor_and_HMS_Gannet_at_Chatham_Dockyard.jpg/250px-Anchor_and_HMS_Gannet_at_Chatham_Dockyard.jpg)](/wiki/File:Anchor_and_HMS_Gannet_at_Chatham_Dockyard.jpg)

[HMS *Gannet*](/wiki/HMS_Gannet_(1878) "HMS Gannet (1878)") at [Chatham Historic Dockyard](/wiki/Chatham_Historic_Dockyard "Chatham Historic Dockyard")

80 acres (0.32 km2), the 18th century core of the site, was transferred to a charity called the [Chatham Historic Dockyard Trust](/wiki/Chatham_Historic_Dockyard "Chatham Historic Dockyard").[[41]](#cite_note-41) The Georgian site is now a visitor attraction under the care of the Trust: [Chatham Historic Dockyard](/wiki/Chatham_Historic_Dockyard "Chatham Historic Dockyard"). The Trust is preparing an application for the dockyard and its defences to become a [World Heritage Site](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site").[[42]](#cite_note-42)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7d/ChathamJulia%26Basin.JPG/250px-ChathamJulia%26Basin.JPG)](/wiki/File:ChathamJulia%26Basin.JPG)

The topsail schooner *Julia* visiting the middle basin in 2006; behind her is the [St Mary's Island](/wiki/St_Mary%27s_Island,_Kent "St Mary's Island, Kent") housing estate

The easternmost basin (Basin No.3) was handed over to the [Medway Ports](/wiki/Medway_Ports "Medway Ports") authority and is now a commercial port (Chatham Docks). It includes Papersafe UK[[43]](#cite_note-43) and Nordic Recycling Ltd.[[44]](#cite_note-44) In 2013 [Peel Ports](/wiki/The_Peel_Group "The Peel Group"), which owns and runs Chatham Docks, announced that it was set to convert a 26-acre (0.11 km2) portion of the commercial port into a mixed-use development (incorporating offices, an education facility, apartments, town houses and a food store ([Asda](/wiki/Asda "Asda")), as well as landscaped public areas). The development is called "Chatham Waters".[[45]](#cite_note-45) In 2019 Peel announced that Chatham Docks would close in 2025 with the loss of 800 jobs.[[46]](#cite_note-46)

The remaining 350 acres (1.4 km2) were transferred to the government's urban regeneration agency (later [English Partnerships](/wiki/English_Partnerships "English Partnerships")). Under its remit, the westernmost (No.1) Basin was turned into a [marina](/wiki/Marina "Marina"), part of the former factory area to the south was transformed into an entertainment and retail complex ('Chatham Maritime') and the former Barracks (HMS *Pembroke*) became [Universities at Medway](/wiki/Universities_at_Medway "Universities at Medway").[[47]](#cite_note-47) [St Mary's Island](/wiki/St_Mary%27s_Island,_Medway "St Mary's Island, Medway"), a 150-acre (0.61 km2), largely undeveloped area to the north of the three basins, was transformed into a residential community for some 1,500 homes. It has several themed areas with traditional maritime buildings, a fishing (though in looks only) village with its multi-coloured houses and a modern energy-efficient concept. Many homes have views of the River Medway. A primary school (St. Mary's C of E) and a [medical centre](/wiki/Clinic "Clinic") provide facilities for the residents and there are attractive walks around the Island.[[48]](#cite_note-48)

#### Filming

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chatham_Dockyard&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Filming")]

Chatham Dockyard has become a popular location for filming, due to its varied and interesting areas such as the cobbled streets, church and over 100 buildings dating from the Georgian and Victorian periods. Productions that have chosen to film at Chatham Dockyard include: *[Les Misérables](/wiki/Les_Mis%C3%A9rables_(2012_film) "Les Misérables (2012 film)")*,[[49]](#cite_note-49) *[Call the Midwife](/wiki/Call_the_Midwife "Call the Midwife")*,[[50]](#cite_note-50) *[Mr Selfridge](/wiki/Mr_Selfridge "Mr Selfridge")*,[[51]](#cite_note-51) *[Sherlock Holmes: A Game of Shadows](/wiki/Sherlock_Holmes:_A_Game_of_Shadows "Sherlock Holmes: A Game of Shadows")*,[[52]](#cite_note-52) *[Oliver Twist](/wiki/Oliver_Twist_(2007_TV_miniseries) "Oliver Twist (2007 TV miniseries)")*,[[53]](#cite_note-53) *[The World Is Not Enough](/wiki/The_World_Is_Not_Enough "The World Is Not Enough")* [[54]](#cite_note-54) and *[Grantchester](/wiki/Grantchester_(TV_series) "Grantchester (TV series)")*.[[55]](#cite_note-55)

## Detailed descriptions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chatham_Dockyard&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Detailed descriptions")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/24/Police_Section_House%2C_Chatham_Dockyard.jpg/250px-Police_Section_House%2C_Chatham_Dockyard.jpg)](/wiki/File:Police_Section_House,_Chatham_Dockyard.jpg)

Police Section House, one of the Dockyard's many [listed buildings](/wiki/Listed_buildings "Listed buildings")

### Significant buildings within the Georgian Dockyard

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chatham_Dockyard&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Significant buildings within the Georgian Dockyard")]

#### Wood and canvas

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chatham_Dockyard&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Wood and canvas")]

* The Mast Ponds. 1697, 1702. [Fir](/wiki/Fir "Fir") logs were [seasoned](/wiki/Wood_drying#Water_seasoning "Wood drying") by immersing them in [salt water](/wiki/Seawater "Seawater") while the [sap](/wiki/Sap "Sap") died back.
  + South Mast Pond, 1697. Now a car park.[[18]](#cite_note-guide27-18)
  + North Mast Pond, 1702. The ponds were connected by canal.[[18]](#cite_note-guide27-18)
* Clocktower building 1723. The oldest surviving naval storehouse in any Royal Dockyard. The building functioned as a "present use store" except for the upper floor, which was a [mould loft](/wiki/Mould_loft "Mould loft"), and six bays at the northern end of the ground floor, which were open and contained [saw pits](/wiki/Saw_pit "Saw pit"). The upper floors had [timber cladding](/wiki/Weatherboarding "Weatherboarding") until 1802 when it was replaced by brick. In the 20th century the building was used for offices; it was adapted in 1996–1997 to become the [University of Kent](/wiki/University_of_Kent "University of Kent")'s Bridge Warden's College.[[56]](#cite_note-guide14-56)[[57]](#cite_note-57)
* Sail and Colour Loft 1723. Constructed from timber recycled from warships probably from the [Dutch Wars](/wiki/Dutch_Wars "Dutch Wars"). Lower floors were for storage, and the upper floor is a large open space for sail construction. In 1758 there were 45 [sailmakers](/wiki/Sailmaker "Sailmaker"). They sewed 2 ft (0.61 m) strips of canvas into the sails using 170–190 stitches per yard (5 stitches per inch), remembering that there would be two rows of stitching to each seam. Flags denoting nationality and for [signals](/wiki/International_maritime_signal_flags "International maritime signal flags") were made here. The flags used by [Nelson](/wiki/Horatio_Nelson,_1st_Viscount_Nelson "Horatio Nelson, 1st Viscount Nelson") in his ["England expects..."](/wiki/England_expects_that_every_man_will_do_his_duty "England expects that every man will do his duty") message would have been made here.[[58]](#cite_note-guide23-58) Flag-making continued here, even after the closure of the dockyard, until 2001.[[59]](#cite_note-59)
* House Carpenter's Workshop c. 1740 House carpenters and joiners were responsible for the fittings and furnishings of warships, and also for building and maintaining various structures within the dockyard itself.[[60]](#cite_note-Coad1989-60) The small courtyard to the west contained workshops.[[61]](#cite_note-61)
* [Masthouses](/wiki/Mast_(sailing) "Mast (sailing)") and [Mouldloft](/wiki/Mould_loft "Mould loft") 1753–1755. Grade I listed since August 1999.[[62]](#cite_note-62) These were used to make and store masts. Here there are seven interlinking masthouses; they were originally connected to the adjacent South Mast Pond by a wide slipway. Above them is the mould loft where the lines of [HMS *Victory*](/wiki/HMS_Victory "HMS Victory") were laid down: the lines of each frame of a ship would be taken from the plan and scribed, full size, into the floor by shipwrights; from this, patterns or moulds would be built using softwoods, and from these the actual frames would be built and shaped. This building houses the "Wooden Walls Exhibition".[[63]](#cite_note-guide8-63)
* Timber Seasoning Sheds 1774. These were built to a standard design with bays 45 by 20 feet (13.7 by 6.1 m). These are the first standardised industrial buildings. There were 75 bays erected at Chatham Dockyard, to hold three years worth of timber.[[64]](#cite_note-guide26-64)
* [Wheelwrights](/wiki/Wheelwright "Wheelwright")' shop circa 1780. This three bay building was originally built as a [mast](/wiki/Mast_(sailing) "Mast (sailing)") house using timber "reclaimed" from dismantled ships. The east bay was used by the wheelwrights, who constructed and repaired the wheels on the dockyard carts, and may have made ships' wheels. The middle bay was used by the [pump](/wiki/Pump "Pump") makers and the coak and [treenail](/wiki/Treenail "Treenail") makers. Pumps were simple affairs, made of wood with iron and leather fittings. Coaks were the bearings in pulley blocks, and treenails were the long oak pins, made on a [lathe](/wiki/Lathe "Lathe") (or 'moot'), that were used to pin the planking to the frames. The west bay was used by the [capstan](/wiki/Capstan_(nautical) "Capstan (nautical)") makers, capstans were used to raise the anchor.[[65]](#cite_note-guide9-65) Since 2017 this building has been linked to the Masthouses and Mouldloft (qv) by a new entrance building for Chatham Historic Dockyard.
* [Joiners](/wiki/Joiner "Joiner")' Shop c. 1790 originally to make treenails, but later used by the yards joiners. The [*Resolute* Desk](/wiki/Resolute_Desk "Resolute Desk") (the Oval Office desk) was constructed here by Dockyard Joiners from the timbers of [HMS *Resolute*](/wiki/HMS_Resolute_(1850) "HMS Resolute (1850)").[[65]](#cite_note-guide9-65)
* Brunel Saw Mill 1814. Until 1814 timber was cut by pairs of men, one above and one below the log. In 1758, there were 43 pairs of [sawyers](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/sawyer "wikt:sawyer") working in the yard. In 1812, the [sawmill](/wiki/Sawmill "Sawmill") was designed by Marc Brunel, father of [Isambard Kingdom Brunel](/wiki/Isambard_Kingdom_Brunel "Isambard Kingdom Brunel"). The mill was driven by [steam](/wiki/Steam "Steam"). The mill was linked to the mast ponds by a mechanical timber transport system, and underground canals. Later the basement was converted into a steam laundry.[[64]](#cite_note-guide26-64)
* Lower Boat House circa 1820 built as a storehouse for squared timber, and later to store ship's boats.[[18]](#cite_note-guide27-18)

#### Dry docks and covered slips

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chatham_Dockyard&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Dry docks and covered slips")]

* The covered slips 1838–1855. It was on [slipways](/wiki/Slipway "Slipway") that ships were built. The slipways were covered, to prevent ships rotting before they had been launched. The earliest covered slips no longer exist (Nos 1 and 2 Slips, which stood either side of the Assistant Queen's Harbourmaster's Office, were given wooden covers in 1817). By 1838 the use of [cast](/wiki/Cast_iron "Cast iron") and [wrought iron](/wiki/Wrought_iron "Wrought iron") in buildings had become feasible. Of those that survive, the oldest slip has a wooden roof, three have cast iron roofing and the latest uses wrought iron; together, they are of unique importance in showing the development of wide span structures such as were later used by the railways.
  + No 3 Slip 1838. This had a linked [timber roof truss](/wiki/Timber_roof_truss "Timber roof truss") structure and was originally covered in [tarred](/wiki/Tar "Tar") paper, which was quickly replaced with a zinc roof. The slip was backfilled around 1900 and a steel [mezzanine](/wiki/Mezzanine_(architecture) "Mezzanine (architecture)") floor was added. It became a store house for ship's boats.[[66]](#cite_note-guide10-66)
  + No 4, 5 and 6 Slips 1848. These were designed by Captain [Thomas Mould](/wiki/Thomas_Rawlings_Mould "Thomas Rawlings Mould"), [Royal Engineers](/wiki/Royal_Engineers "Royal Engineers"), and erected by Bakers and Sons of Lambeth. Similar structures were erected at [Portsmouth](/wiki/Portsmouth "Portsmouth") but these are no longer extant. They predate the London [train sheds](/wiki/Train_shed "Train shed") of [Paddington](/wiki/Paddington "Paddington") and [King's Cross](/wiki/London_King%27s_Cross_railway_station "London King's Cross railway station") which were often cited as the country's first wide span metal structures.[[66]](#cite_note-guide10-66)
  + No 7 Slip is one of the earliest examples of a modern metal [trussed](/wiki/Truss "Truss") roof. It was designed in 1852 by Colonel Godfrey T. Green, [Royal Engineers](/wiki/Royal_Engineers "Royal Engineers"). It was used for shipbuilding until 1966; [HMS *Ocelot*](/wiki/HMS_Ocelot "HMS Ocelot") was launched from there on 5 May 1962.[[67]](#cite_note-guide12-67)
* [Dry Dock](/wiki/Dry_Dock "Dry Dock"). The docks are filled by [sluice gates](/wiki/Sluice_gate "Sluice gate") set into the [caissons](/wiki/Caisson_(engineering) "Caisson (engineering)"), and emptied by a series of underground [culverts](/wiki/Culvert "Culvert") connected to the [pumping station](/wiki/Pumping_station "Pumping station"). No 1 Dry Dock 1824 (built on the site of "The Old Double Dock") no longer exists; it was filled in and converted into a covered workshop (No 1 Machine Shop) prior to the pioneering construction of [HMS *Achilles*](/wiki/HMS_Achilles_(1863) "HMS Achilles (1863)") in the adjacent No 2 Dock.
  + No 2 Dry Dock 1856 was built on the site of "The Old Single Dock" where HMS *Victory* was constructed. In 1863, this dock constructed [HMS *Achilles*](/wiki/HMS_Achilles_(1863) "HMS Achilles (1863)"), the first iron [battleship](/wiki/Battleship "Battleship") to be built in a Royal Dockyard. It now houses [HMS *Cavalier*](/wiki/HMS_Cavalier "HMS Cavalier").[[56]](#cite_note-guide14-56)
  + No 3 Dry Dock 1820, the first to be constructed of stone, was designed by [John Rennie](/wiki/John_Rennie_the_Elder "John Rennie the Elder"). It now houses HMS *Ocelot*.[[56]](#cite_note-guide14-56)
  + No 4 Dry Dock 1840 was built on the site of one of a parallel pair of docks built in 1686 (the second of which was converted into the adjacent slipway, No. 3 Slip). It now houses [HMS *Gannet*](/wiki/HMS_Gannet_(1878) "HMS Gannet (1878)").[[56]](#cite_note-guide14-56)
* South Dock Pumping Station 1822, designed by [John Rennie](/wiki/John_Rennie_the_Elder "John Rennie the Elder"). It originally housed a [beam engine](/wiki/Beam_engine "Beam engine"), which was replaced by an electric [pump](/wiki/Pump "Pump") in 1920. The building is still in use.[[56]](#cite_note-guide14-56)

#### Offices and residential

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chatham_Dockyard&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Offices and residential")]

* Commissioner's House 1704. This is the oldest surviving naval building in England. It is [Grade I listed](/wiki/Grade_I_listed "Grade I listed").[[68]](#cite_note-68) It was built for the Resident Commissioner, his family and servants. The previous building was built in 1640 for [Phineas Pett](/wiki/Phineas_Pett "Phineas Pett"). In 1703, Captain [George St Lo](/wiki/George_St_Lo "George St Lo") took up the post and petitioned [the Admiralty](/wiki/The_Admiralty "The Admiralty") for a more suitable residence. Internally the principal feature is the main staircase with its painted wooden ceiling attributed to Thomas Highmore ([Serjeant Painter](/wiki/Serjeant_Painter "Serjeant Painter")), to sketches by [Sir James Thornhill](/wiki/Sir_James_Thornhill "Sir James Thornhill").[[16]](#cite_note-guide15-16)
* Commissioner's Garden dating from 1640. The lower terraces are one of the first [Italianate](/wiki/Italianate_architecture "Italianate architecture") [Water Gardens](/wiki/Water_garden "Water garden") in England. There is a 400-year-old [mulberry](/wiki/Mulberry "Mulberry") tree, from where [Oliver Cromwell](/wiki/Oliver_Cromwell "Oliver Cromwell") reputedly watched the [Roundhead](/wiki/Roundhead "Roundhead") Army take [Rochester](/wiki/Rochester,_Kent "Rochester, Kent") from the [Royalists](/wiki/Cavalier "Cavalier"). There is an 18th-century [icehouse](/wiki/Ice_house_(building) "Ice house (building)") and an [Edwardian](/wiki/Edwardian_era "Edwardian era") [conservatory](/wiki/Conservatory_(greenhouse) "Conservatory (greenhouse)") with its great [vine](/wiki/Vine "Vine").[[69]](#cite_note-guide16-69)
* Officers' Terrace 1722–23. Twelve houses built for senior officers in the Dockyard. The ground floor were built as offices, the first floor contained reception rooms with bedrooms above. Each has an 18th-century [walled garden](/wiki/Walled_garden "Walled garden"), which again are now very rare. They are now privately owned.[[70]](#cite_note-guide25-70)
* House [Carpenters](/wiki/Carpenter "Carpenter")' Shop c. 1740. Built to harmonise with the officers' terrace. House Carpenters worked solely on maintaining the dockyard buildings.[[70]](#cite_note-guide25-70)
* Stables. For officers' horses.[[58]](#cite_note-guide23-58)
* Main gatehouse 1722, designed by the master shipwright in the style of [John Vanbrugh](/wiki/John_Vanbrugh "John Vanbrugh"). It bears the arms of [George III](/wiki/George_III "George III"). Inside the gateway stands the *muster bell* on a wrought iron mast dating from the late 18th or early 19th century; it is [Grade II\* listed](/wiki/Grade_II*_listed "Grade II* listed").[[71]](#cite_note-71)[[72]](#cite_note-guide22-72)
* Guard House 1764. Built when [Marines](/wiki/Marine_(military) "Marine (military)") were introduced into the Dockyard to improve security. It continued in use till 1984.[[58]](#cite_note-guide23-58)
* Cashiers' Office 18th century. The Pay Office was moved here in 1750 from Hill House,[[10]](#cite_note-TheHillHouse-10) and remained here until the yard closed. John Dickens, father of [Charles Dickens](/wiki/Charles_Dickens "Charles Dickens"), worked here from 1817 to 1822. It is still used as offices.[[70]](#cite_note-guide25-70)
* Assistant Queen's [Harbourmaster](/wiki/Harbourmaster "Harbourmaster")'s Office c. 1770, Grade II\* listed.[[73]](#cite_note-73) This office was supplied to the person who has been appointed to superintend the Dockyard Port. In 1865, the whole of the tidal [Medway](/wiki/River_Medway "River Medway") from [Allington Lock](/wiki/Allington,_Kent "Allington, Kent") to [Sheerness](/wiki/Sheerness "Sheerness") was designated as a dockyard port and the Assistant Queen's Harbourmaster was responsible for all moorings and movements. Alongside this office is a set of stone steps leading into the river Medway, with a wrought iron arch and lantern holder. Also Grade II listed.[[74]](#cite_note-74) This was called the "Queen Stairs"[[75]](#cite_note-75) and was the formal entry into the dockyard, during the "Age of Sail".[[69]](#cite_note-guide16-69)
* Dockyard Church 1806. Designed by Edward Holl, it has a gallery supported on cast iron columns, one of the first uses of cast iron in the dockyard. Last used in 1981.[[58]](#cite_note-guide23-58)
* [Admiral](/wiki/Admiral "Admiral")'s Offices 1808. Designed by [Edward Holl](/wiki/Edward_Holl "Edward Holl") as offices for the master [shipwright](/wiki/Shipwright "Shipwright"). The roofline was low so it would not obstruct the view from the officers' terrace. Later it became [Port Admiral](/wiki/Port_Admiral_(Royal_Navy) "Port Admiral (Royal Navy)")'s office and was extended. The northern extension became the dockyard's communication centre.[[16]](#cite_note-guide15-16)
* Thunderbolt Pier, a pier named after [HMS *Thunderbolt*](/wiki/HMS_Thunderbolt "HMS Thunderbolt"), built 1856, which was used as a floating [pier](/wiki/Pier "Pier") from 1873 until 1948, when she was rammed and sunk.[[76]](#cite_note-76)
* Captain of the Dockyard's House 19th century. Now a private residence. Also Grade II\* listed.[[77]](#cite_note-77)[[78]](#cite_note-guide24-78)

#### Anchor Wharf and the Ropery

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chatham_Dockyard&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Anchor Wharf and the Ropery")]

* Anchor Wharf Store Houses 1778–1805 (at nearly 700 feet [210 m] long) are the largest storehouses ever built for the navy.[[79]](#cite_note-storehouse-79)
  + The southern building, Store House No 3, completed in 1785, is subdivided with timber lattice partitions as a "lay apart store", a store for equipment from vessels under repair. It has been Grade I listed since August 1999.[[79]](#cite_note-storehouse-79)
  + The northern building was used as a fitted rigging house, and a general store for equipment to fit out newly built ships. It also has been Grade I listed since August 1999.[[80]](#cite_note-80) The Fitted Rigging House is now used as the Library and houses the Steam Steel and Submarines 1832–1984 gallery.[[81]](#cite_note-guide17-81)
* The Ropery consists of [Hemp](/wiki/Hemp "Hemp") Houses (1728, extended 1812), Yarn Houses and a double Rope House with attached Hatchelling House. Hatchelling is combing the hemp fibres to straighten them out before spinning. This was the first stage of the ropemaking process. The Ropery is still in use, being operated by Master Ropemakers Ltd.[[82]](#cite_note-82)
  + The Double Rope House has [spinning](/wiki/Spinning_(textiles) "Spinning (textiles)") on the upper floors and ropemaking (a [ropewalk](/wiki/Ropewalk "Ropewalk")) on the ground floor.[[83]](#cite_note-guide18-83) It is 346 m (1,135 ft) long, and when constructed was the longest brick-built building in Europe capable of laying a 1,000 ft (300 m) [rope](/wiki/Rope "Rope"). Over 200 men were required before 1836, to make and lay a 20in (circumference) [cable](/wiki/Wire_rope "Wire rope"). All was done by hand. [Steam power](/wiki/Steam_power "Steam power") in the form of a [beam engine](/wiki/Beam_engine "Beam engine") was introduced in 1836, and then electricity in the early 1900s.
  + The White Yarn House to store the yarn before it was tarred to prevent rot.
  + The [Tarring](/wiki/Tar "Tar") House with its "Tar Kettle" and horse drawn [winch](/wiki/Winch "Winch").
  + The Black Yarn House to store the tarred yarn. The tarring process declined as [manila](/wiki/Manila_hemp "Manila hemp") replaced [hemp](/wiki/Hemp "Hemp"), and [sisal](/wiki/Sisal "Sisal") replaced manila. These fibres were chemically protected at the hatchelling stage and tarring stopped in the 1940s.

#### Metalwork

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chatham_Dockyard&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Metalwork")]

* No 1 [Smithery](/wiki/Smithery "Smithery") 1808. Originally consisting of three ranges around an open courtyard, it was designed by Edward Holl and fitted out by John Rennie with 40 forges for production of [Anchors](/wiki/Anchors "Anchors") and [Chain](/wiki/Chain "Chain"). Anchors could weigh 72 hundredweight (3,658 kg), and were forged by hand. "Anchorsmiths" were given an allowance of 8 imperial pints (4.5 L) of strong beer a day, because of the difficult working conditions.[[64]](#cite_note-guide26-64) In 1841 a beam engine was installed (in an extension to the north-east) to replace the manual bellows; another was added the following year.[[24]](#cite_note-SmitheryListing-24) The courtyard was roofed over for a steam hammer shop in 1865.[[24]](#cite_note-SmitheryListing-24)
* Lead and [Paint](/wiki/Paint "Paint") Mill 1818. Designed by Edward Holl to be fireproof. There were a lead [furnace](/wiki/Metallurgical_furnace "Metallurgical furnace"), [casting](/wiki/Casting "Casting") area and steam powered double [rolling mill](/wiki/Rolling_mill "Rolling mill"), paint mills for grinding pigment, [canvas](/wiki/Canvas "Canvas") stretching frames, and vats for storing and boiling [linseed oil](/wiki/Linseed_oil "Linseed oil"). A warship was painted every 4 months.[[72]](#cite_note-guide22-72)
* Iron Foundry 1855–61. Built immediately to the north of the Smithery (the intervening space was later infilled with the Smithery extension of 1867). Extended in connection with preparations for HMS *Achilles* (see below).[[24]](#cite_note-SmitheryListing-24)
* No 1 Machine Shop 1861. This building retains it original structure and roof glazing. It was used to house the [machine tools](/wiki/Machine_tools "Machine tools") needed to produce [HMS *Achilles*](/wiki/HMS_Achilles_(1863) "HMS Achilles (1863)"), the first iron [battleship](/wiki/Battleship "Battleship") built in a Royal Dockyard. This building has now become home to the railway workshop.[[56]](#cite_note-guide14-56)
* The [Galvanising](/wiki/Galvanising "Galvanising") Shop c. 1890. Galvanising is a process of dipping steel in molten [zinc](/wiki/Zinc "Zinc") to prevent it from [rusting](/wiki/Rust "Rust"). There were baths of [acid](/wiki/Acid "Acid") and molten zinc, the fumes vented through louvres in the roof.[[64]](#cite_note-guide26-64)
* Chain Cable Shed c. 1900, built to protect newly manufactured [anchor](/wiki/Anchor "Anchor") [chain](/wiki/Chain "Chain"). The roof is supported by a row of 28 captured French and Spanish guns.[[72]](#cite_note-guide22-72)

### Surviving structures within the Victorian Steam Yard

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chatham_Dockyard&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Surviving structures within the Victorian Steam Yard")]

The Victorian Steam Yard was built around three large [Basins](/wiki/Wet_dock "Wet dock") ([wet docks](/wiki/Wet_dock "Wet dock")), constructed between 1865 and 1885 along the line of St Mary's Creek (separating [St Mary's Island](/wiki/St_Mary%27s_Island,_Medway "St Mary's Island, Medway") from the mainland). It was envisaged that Basin No 1 would serve as a "repair basin", No 2 as the "factory basin" and No 3 as the "[fitting-out](/wiki/Fitting-out "Fitting-out")" basin; a newly launched ship could therefore enter via the west lock, have any defects remedied in the first basin, have her [steam engines](/wiki/Steam_engines "Steam engines") and heavy machinery installed in the second, and then be finished, and loaded with coal and provisions, in the third before leaving via the east locks.[[20]](#cite_note-Coad2013-20)

* Four [drydocks](/wiki/Drydocks "Drydocks") (Nos 5–8) were constructed at the same time on the south side of No 1 Basin; all four were in use by 1873. The yard's Steam Factory complex was built at around the same time; most of its buildings were sited around these docks (rather than by Basin No 2 as had originally been planned).[[84]](#cite_note-guide30-84)
* No 1 Boiler Shop and No 8 Machine Shop were originally built as slip covers at [Woolwich Dockyard](/wiki/Woolwich_Dockyard "Woolwich Dockyard") in the 1840s. When that Dockyard closed in 1869 they were dismantled and rebuilt at Chatham alongside the new dry docks. Only the framework survives of the Machine Shop, but the Boiler Shop was renovated in 2003 to house the Dockyard Outlet shopping centre. A third such structure from Woolwich, similar in design to the boiler shop, stood nearby and served as a fitting shop; it was demolished in 1990.[[85]](#cite_note-85)
* Dock Pumping Station 1874: as well as serving to empty dry docks 5–8 when required, its [accumulator tower](/wiki/Accumulator_tower "Accumulator tower") provided hydraulic power for the adjacent cranes, [capstans](/wiki/Capstan_(nautical) "Capstan (nautical)") and [caissons](/wiki/Caisson_lock "Caisson lock").[[86]](#cite_note-86) Two other pumping stations served a similar purpose (one for dock 9 and one for the two east locks) but they have not survived.[[20]](#cite_note-Coad2013-20)
* Combined Ship Trade Office 1880: now the "Ship & Trades" public house.[[87]](#cite_note-87)
* A fifth dry dock (No 9) was added in 1895 on the north side of No 1 Basin, opposite the other four, to accommodate the new, larger battleships which were then under construction. It was completed in 1903.[[88]](#cite_note-88)
* The 100 ft [bell mast](/wiki/HMS_Undaunted_(1861)#Bell_mast "HMS Undaunted (1861)") was erected in 1903 alongside the Dockyard's Pembroke Gate, where it was used to signal the start of each working day. (A similar but older mast fulfilled the same function alongside the main gate in the Georgian part of the Yard.) The 1903 mast had originally served as foremast to [HMS *Undaunted*](/wiki/HMS_Undaunted_(1861) "HMS Undaunted (1861)"). In 1992 it had been dismantled, but was re-erected, a short distance from its original location, in 2001. Apart from the two Chatham examples, only one other is believed to have survived: in Devonport's [Morice Ordnance Yard](/wiki/HMNB_Devonport "HMNB Devonport").[[89]](#cite_note-89)

## Administration of the dockyard

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chatham_Dockyard&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Administration of the dockyard")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/21/ChathamHDYCommissioners0004.JPG/250px-ChathamHDYCommissioners0004.JPG)](/wiki/File:ChathamHDYCommissioners0004.JPG)

The Commissioner's House (1704), was built for Captain [George St Lo](/wiki/George_St_Lo "George St Lo"), who found the previous house unsuitable. It remains the oldest surviving naval building in England.

### Resident Commissioners of the Navy Board

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chatham_Dockyard&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Resident Commissioners of the Navy Board")]

## The Gun Wharf

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chatham_Dockyard&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: The Gun Wharf")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c5/ChathamMedway2657.JPG/250px-ChathamMedway2657.JPG)](/wiki/File:ChathamMedway2657.JPG)

The Ordnance Storekeeper's house at the heart of the former Gun Wharf

An [Ordnance Yard](/wiki/Board_of_Ordnance "Board of Ordnance") was established in the 17th century immediately upriver of the Dockyard (on the site of the original [Tudor](/wiki/Tudor_era "Tudor era") yard, vacated in 1622). The yard would have received, stored and issued cannons and gun-carriages (along with projectiles, accoutrements and also all manner of small arms) for ships based in the Medway, as well as for local artillery emplacements and for army use. (Gunpowder, on the other hand, was received, stored and issued across the river at [Upnor Castle](/wiki/Upnor_Castle "Upnor Castle").)[[121]](#cite_note-HeritageSaunders1985-121)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bd/The_Medway%2C_Chatham%2C_England-LCCN2002711859.tif/lossy-page1-250px-The_Medway%2C_Chatham%2C_England-LCCN2002711859.tif.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Medway,_Chatham,_England-LCCN2002711859.tif)

The Gun Wharf, c. 1890: the 1717 Grand Store can be seen left of centre (with the Dockyard's Anchor Wharf storehouse in the distance beyond). The surviving carpenters' shop and machine shop are on the right.

A plan of 1704 shows (from north to south) a long Storehouse parallel to the river, the Storekeeper's house (the Storekeeper was the senior officer of the Yard) and a pair of Carriage Stores. In 1717 the original Storehouse was replaced with the Grand Store (a much larger three-storey building, contemporary with and of a similar style to, the Main Gatehouse in the Dockyard). Not long afterwards a large new single-storey Carriage Store, with a long frontage parallel to the river, was constructed, adjoining the Storekeeper's House to the south.[[122]](#cite_note-122)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f3/Chatham_Library%2C_Gun_Wharf%2C_Chatham_-_geograph.org.uk_-_674832.jpg/250px-Chatham_Library%2C_Gun_Wharf%2C_Chatham_-_geograph.org.uk_-_674832.jpg)](/wiki/File:Chatham_Library,_Gun_Wharf,_Chatham_-_geograph.org.uk_-_674832.jpg)

The Library (former machine shop)

After the demise of the Board of Ordnance (1855), Ordnance Yards passed under the control of the War Office, and were eventually (in 1891) apportioned to either the Army or the Navy. Chatham's yard was split in two, the area south of the Storekeeper's House becoming an Army Ordnance Store, and the rest a Navy Ordnance Store. It remained thus until 1958 when the yards were closed (the Army depot having served latterly as an atomic weapons research laboratory).[[123]](#cite_note-123) Most of the 18th-century buildings were demolished, with the exception of the Storekeeper's House of 1719, which survives as the Command House [public house](/wiki/Public_house "Public house").[[124]](#cite_note-124) A few later buildings have survived: a long brick shed of 1805, southwest of the Command House, which once housed carpenters, wheelwrights and other workers as well as stores of various kinds,[[125]](#cite_note-125) the adjacent building ([machine shop](/wiki/Machine_shop "Machine shop"), late-19th century) which now serves as a public library, and the building known as the White House (built as the Clerk of the Cheque's residence in 1816).[[20]](#cite_note-Coad2013-20)

## Defence of the dockyard

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chatham_Dockyard&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Defence of the dockyard")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/44/Plan_of_Chatham_Lines_-_Thomas_Chamberlaine%2C_1804_-_BL_Add_MS_11060.jpg/250px-Plan_of_Chatham_Lines_-_Thomas_Chamberlaine%2C_1804_-_BL_Add_MS_11060.jpg)](/wiki/File:Plan_of_Chatham_Lines_-_Thomas_Chamberlaine,_1804_-_BL_Add_MS_11060.jpg)

Plan of Chatham Lines, 1804: fortification protecting the dockyard (below centre); Upnor Castle is in the bottom left corner.

### Upnor Castle

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chatham_Dockyard&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Upnor Castle")]

Main article: [Upnor Castle](/wiki/Upnor_Castle "Upnor Castle")

Dockyards have always required shore defences. Among the earliest for Chatham was [Upnor Castle](/wiki/Upnor_Castle "Upnor Castle"), built in 1567, on the opposite side of the [River Medway](/wiki/River_Medway "River Medway"). It was somewhat unfortunate that, on the one occasion when it was required for action—the [Raid on the Medway](/wiki/Raid_on_the_Medway "Raid on the Medway"), 1667—the Dutch fleet were able to sail right past it to attack the English fleet and carry off the pride of the fleet, [HMS *Royal Charles*](/wiki/HMS_Royal_Charles_(1660) "HMS Royal Charles (1660)"), back to the Netherlands.[[126]](#cite_note-126)

### Chain defence

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chatham_Dockyard&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Chain defence")]

During the wars with Spain it was usual for ships to anchor at Chatham in reserve; consequently [John Hawkins](/wiki/John_Hawkins_(naval_commander) "John Hawkins (naval commander)") threw a [massive chain](/wiki/Boom_(navigational_barrier) "Boom (navigational barrier)") across the River Medway for extra defence in 1585. Hawkins' chain was later replaced with a [boom](/wiki/Boom_(navigational_barrier) "Boom (navigational barrier)") of masts, iron, cordage, and the hulls of two old ships, besides a couple of ruined pinnacles.[[127]](#cite_note-127)

### The Lines

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chatham_Dockyard&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: The Lines")]

See also: [Great Lines Heritage Park](/wiki/Great_Lines_Heritage_Park "Great Lines Heritage Park")

With the failure of Upnor Castle, it was seen necessary to increase the defences. In the event, those defences were built in distinct phases, as the government saw the increasing threat of invasion:[[128]](#cite_note-Bromponlines-128)

* 1669 [Fort Gillingham](/wiki/Fort_Gillingham "Fort Gillingham") and [Cockham Wood Fort](/wiki/Cockham_Wood_Fort "Cockham Wood Fort") built.
* 1756 Chatham Lines built, to designs by Captain John Desmaretz (who also designed the Portsmouth fortifications).[[128]](#cite_note-Bromponlines-128) This fortification, and its subsequent upgrading, were to concentrate on an overland attack and so were built to face south. They included redoubts at Amherst and Townsend. The Lines enclosed the entire dockyard on its eastern side.
* 1778–1783 Further improvements were carried out, to the designs of Captain Hugh Debbeig at the bequest of General Amherst. In 1782, an [Act of Parliament](/wiki/Act_of_Parliament "Act of Parliament") increased the land needed for the Field of Fire.[[128]](#cite_note-Bromponlines-128)
* 1805–1812 Amherst redoubt, now [Fort Amherst](/wiki/Fort_Amherst "Fort Amherst"); new forts, named [Pitt](/wiki/Fort_Pitt,_Kent "Fort Pitt, Kent") and [Clarence](/wiki/Fort_Clarence "Fort Clarence"). The Lines were also extended to the east of Saint Mary's Creek (on St Mary's Island).[[128]](#cite_note-Bromponlines-128)
* 1860s [Grain Fort](/wiki/Grain_Fort "Grain Fort"), and other smaller batteries in that area.
* 1870–1892 A number of forts built at a greater distance from the dockyard: Forts [Bridgewood](/wiki/Fort_Bridgewood "Fort Bridgewood"), [Luton](/wiki/Fort_Luton "Fort Luton"), [Borstal](/wiki/Fort_Borstal "Fort Borstal"), [Horsted](/wiki/Fort_Horsted "Fort Horsted") and [Darland](/wiki/Fort_Darland "Fort Darland"). These became known as the "Great Lines". Forts [Darnet](/wiki/Fort_Darnet "Fort Darnet") and [Hoo](/wiki/Fort_Hoo "Fort Hoo") built on islands in the River Medway.

* [![The defences in 1770.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6d/ChathamDefences_1770%282%29.svg/250px-ChathamDefences_1770%282%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:ChathamDefences_1770(2).svg "The defences in 1770.")

  The defences in 1770.
* [![The defences in 1812.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a5/ChathamDefences_1812%282%29.svg/250px-ChathamDefences_1812%282%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:ChathamDefences_1812(2).svg "The defences in 1812.")

  The defences in 1812.
