## Associated barracks

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chatham_Dockyard&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Associated barracks")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4b/Barracks%2C_Upnor_Castle_-_geograph.org.uk_-_3028944.jpg/250px-Barracks%2C_Upnor_Castle_-_geograph.org.uk_-_3028944.jpg)](/wiki/File:Barracks,_Upnor_Castle_-_geograph.org.uk_-_3028944.jpg)

Upnor Castle barracks (1718).

The Dockyard led to large numbers of military personnel being garrisoned in Chatham and the surrounding area. A good many were engaged in manning the defences, but some had other duties; others were accommodated there for convenience prior to embarking on ships for duties overseas, or following their disembarkation. Initially, soldiers were housed under canvas or else billetted in houses and inns, but from the 18th century barracks began to be constructed. The oldest surviving barracks in the Chatham area is in [Upnor](/wiki/Upnor "Upnor"); dating from 1718, it housed the detachment of 64 men responsible for guarding the gunpowder store in [Upnor Castle](/wiki/Upnor_Castle "Upnor Castle").[[121]](#cite_note-HeritageSaunders1985-121)

### Infantry Barracks (Kitchener Barracks)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chatham_Dockyard&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Infantry Barracks (Kitchener Barracks)")]

Main article: [Kitchener Barracks](/wiki/Kitchener_Barracks "Kitchener Barracks")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ef/Brompton_Barracks_%28geograph_5517020%29.jpg/250px-Brompton_Barracks_%28geograph_5517020%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Brompton_Barracks_(geograph_5517020).jpg)

Surviving 1757 block from the original Infantry Barracks

Chatham Infantry Barracks was opened in 1757 to house troops manning the fortifications which had recently been built to defend the Dockyard. Accommodating some 1,800 men, Chatham was one of the first large-scale Army [barracks](/wiki/Barracks "Barracks") in England.[[129]](#cite_note-KBListing-129) Built on sloping ground east of the Dockyard, the rectangular site (enclosed within its own perimeter wall) consisted of a 'Lower Barracks' with accommodation for the soldiers and 'Upper Barracks' (later known as 'The Terrace') which housed the officers; between the two was a large parade ground.[[130]](#cite_note-Douet1998-130) The three-storey barrack blocks were aligned north–south.[[129]](#cite_note-KBListing-129) Externally, all the blocks were similar in appearance. Those for the officers were placed in a single row on elevated ground to the east, accessed from the parade ground by way of a double ramp; they provided housing for two [field officers](/wiki/Field_officer "Field officer"), twelve [captains](/wiki/Captain_(British_Army_and_Royal_Marines) "Captain (British Army and Royal Marines)") and thirty-seven [subalterns](/wiki/Subalterns "Subalterns"). The subalterns were given one room each, the captains two, and field officers four. The officers all had servants, who were provided with their own accommodation in the [garret](/wiki/Garret "Garret"). The barracks for [other ranks](/wiki/Other_ranks_(UK) "Other ranks (UK)") were arranged in three rows, west of the parade ground, with soldiers sleeping sixteen to a room in eight double beds, as was standard practice at that time. Similar but smaller blocks to the north and south housed ancillary services, such as the pay office, infirmary and Quartermaster's stores.[[130]](#cite_note-Douet1998-130)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/20/Barrack_Block%2C_Kitchener_Barracks%2C_Chatham_-_geograph.org.uk_-_577164.jpg/250px-Barrack_Block%2C_Kitchener_Barracks%2C_Chatham_-_geograph.org.uk_-_577164.jpg)](/wiki/File:Barrack_Block,_Kitchener_Barracks,_Chatham_-_geograph.org.uk_-_577164.jpg)

Kitchener Barracks, lower level (the 1950s extension, demolished in 2017).

Within the space of 20 years, Chatham Barracks had taken on an additional role as the Army Depôt of Recruits and Invalids: accommodating 'a mixed mass of people, viz. recruits destined for regiments which were abroad, prisoners confined on account of desertion and other military offences, added to which was a proportion of invalids, sent home from foreign stations on account of old age, or bodily infirmity'.[[131]](#cite_note-Jackson_1819-131) The establishment of the Depôt was linked to the appointment in 1778 of an 'Inspector General and Superintendent of the Recruiting of all the Forces employed on foreign service', based in Chatham, who provided a degree of centralised oversight of [recruitment](/wiki/Recruitment_in_the_British_Army "Recruitment in the British Army") (responsibility for which had previously been left entirely to the regiments).[[132]](#cite_note-Roper1998-132) During this time, Chatham Depôt provided basic training for new recruits. This role ceased in 1801, however, when the entire Depôt establishment moved to [Parkhurst Barracks](/wiki/Albany_Barracks "Albany Barracks") on the [Isle of Wight](/wiki/Isle_of_Wight "Isle of Wight") (where the recruits were less prone to find ways of deserting).[[133]](#cite_note-Glover1963-133)

During the [Napoleonic Wars](/wiki/Napoleonic_Wars "Napoleonic Wars") large numbers of troops were concentrated at Chatham Lines to counter the threat of a French invasion. In the years of peace after 1815 Chatham continued to serve as a major embarkation port for troops serving overseas.[[129]](#cite_note-KBListing-129) The Infantry Barracks went on to serve as a home [depot](/wiki/Regimental_depot "Regimental depot") for numerous regiments.[[134]](#cite_note-134)

A Royal Commission of 1861 (set up in the wake of the Crimean War to improve the sanitary condition of Britain's Army barracks and Hospitals)[[135]](#cite_note-135) was scathing in its judgement of facilities at Chatham; in the years that followed several alterations were made to Chatham Barracks (which had changed little over the preceding century): an additional storey was added to several of the barrack blocks to help alleviate overcrowding, and new buildings were inserted among the old, containing improved facilities for cooking, washing and recreation. At around the same time the officers' blocks on the terrace were given over to provide accommodation for sergeants and married [NCOs](/wiki/Non-commissioned_officer "Non-commissioned officer"), the officers being provided with new quarters 'near the fortifications at Prince Henry's Bastion'.[[136]](#cite_note-PP1867-136) In 1865, following the opening of new and enlarged hospital facilities in [Fort Pitt](/wiki/Fort_Pitt,_Kent "Fort Pitt, Kent"), the old Garrison Hospital was closed; its buildings (on what is now Maxwell Road) were converted into barracks and named 'Upper Chatham Barracks'[[137]](#cite_note-137) (later 'Upper Kitchener Barracks'). New married quarters were also opened in March that same year, into which soldiers' families were moved, mostly from lodgings in the town.[[136]](#cite_note-PP1867-136)

In 1928 Chatham Barracks was taken over by the [Royal Engineers](/wiki/Royal_Engineers "Royal Engineers") and renamed Kitchener Barracks. One barrack block remains from 1757; the rest was largely demolished and rebuilt to a more modern design in the 1930s–50s. The barracks remained in military use until 2014.[[129]](#cite_note-KBListing-129)

In 2014 the site was sold to a property developer, with permission given the following year for the building of 295 homes. The main 1930s barracks building is being retained, along with the remaining earlier structures.[[138]](#cite_note-138) The new development has retained the name Kitchener Barracks.[[139]](#cite_note-139)

### Marine Barracks

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chatham_Dockyard&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Marine Barracks")]

Main article: [Royal Marine Barracks, Chatham](/wiki/Royal_Marine_Barracks,_Chatham "Royal Marine Barracks, Chatham")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ad/The_Royal_Navy_during_the_Second_World_War_A2184.jpg/250px-The_Royal_Navy_during_the_Second_World_War_A2184.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Royal_Navy_during_the_Second_World_War_A2184.jpg)

The Royal Marine Barracks in the Second World War.

The Royal Marine Barracks, Chatham were established in 1779, on a site nestled between the Gun Wharf to the west, the Dockyard to the north, and Infantry Barracks to the east. The site was expanded and rebuilt in the 1860s; in 1905 the Royal Marines took over Melville Barracks, which stood between Dock Road and Brompton Hill (it had formerly served as Chatham's [Royal Naval Hospital](/wiki/Royal_Naval_Hospital "Royal Naval Hospital")). The Marines were withdrawn from Chatham in 1950, and the buildings were later demolished. [Medway Council](/wiki/Medway_Council "Medway Council") offices and car park now stand on the site.[[140]](#cite_note-140)

### Artillery/Engineer Barracks (Brompton Barracks)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chatham_Dockyard&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Artillery/Engineer Barracks (Brompton Barracks)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5c/HQ_Royal_School_of_Military_Engineering.jpg/250px-HQ_Royal_School_of_Military_Engineering.jpg)](/wiki/File:HQ_Royal_School_of_Military_Engineering.jpg)

Royal School of Military Engineering (1872) and Boer War Memorial Arch (1902) at Brompton Barracks.

A barracks was built in [Brompton](/wiki/Brompton,_Kent "Brompton, Kent") from 1804 to 1806 for the [Royal Artillery](/wiki/Royal_Artillery "Royal Artillery") gunners serving on the defensive Lines (previously they had been accommodated in the Infantry Barracks). There was space for some 500 horses and 1,000 men. In 1812 the [Royal Engineers Establishment](/wiki/Royal_School_of_Military_Engineering "Royal School of Military Engineering") was founded within the barracks to provide instruction in [military engineering](/wiki/Military_engineering "Military engineering"). The Establishment grew, and by 1856 the Artillery had moved out; Brompton Barracks remains in service as headquarters of the Royal Engineers.[[141]](#cite_note-141)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/98/The_Garrison_Church_of_Saint_Barbara%2C_Maxwell_Road%2C_Brompton_%281%29_-_geograph.org.uk_-_517097.jpg/250px-The_Garrison_Church_of_Saint_Barbara%2C_Maxwell_Road%2C_Brompton_%281%29_-_geograph.org.uk_-_517097.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Garrison_Church_of_Saint_Barbara,_Maxwell_Road,_Brompton_(1)_-_geograph.org.uk_-_517097.jpg)

The Garrison Church of St Barbara in Maxwell Road continues to serve Brompton Barracks.

### St Mary's Barracks

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chatham_Dockyard&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: St Mary's Barracks")]

St Mary's [Casemated](/wiki/Casemate "Casemate") Barracks were built during the [Peninsular War](/wiki/Peninsular_War "Peninsular War") and initially held French [prisoners of war](/wiki/Prisoners_of_war "Prisoners of war"). After the war's end, they went on to serve as a gunpowder store for a time, and were used by the Royal Engineers (based nearby in Brompton Barracks). From 1844 St Mary's was used as an 'Invalid Barracks', accommodating soldiers having to return from service in different parts of the British Empire because of illness, injury or age.[[142]](#cite_note-142) Built within the defensive earthworks to the north of Chatham, St Mary's Barracks was demolished in the 1960s and the land used for housing.[[143]](#cite_note-143)

### Naval Barracks (HMS *Pembroke*)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chatham_Dockyard&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Naval Barracks (HMS Pembroke)")]

Main article: [Royal Naval Barracks, Chatham](/wiki/Royal_Naval_Barracks,_Chatham "Royal Naval Barracks, Chatham")

See also: [Drill Hall Library](/wiki/Drill_Hall_Library "Drill Hall Library")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/29/University_of_Greenwich%2C_Pembroke._-_geograph.org.uk_-_584932.jpg/250px-University_of_Greenwich%2C_Pembroke._-_geograph.org.uk_-_584932.jpg)](/wiki/File:University_of_Greenwich,_Pembroke._-_geograph.org.uk_-_584932.jpg)

HMS *Pembroke*: former officers' quarters

The Naval Barracks (later HMS *Pembroke*) opened in 1902; prior to this, most Naval (as opposed to Dockyard) personnel were accommodated on board their ships or on [hulks](/wiki/Hulk_(ship_type) "Hulk (ship type)") moored nearby. Built on the site of what had been the convict prison, the barracks complex could accommodate 4,742 officers and seamen in a series of large blocks built along the length of a terrace. Below the terrace lay the parade ground and its adjacent drill hall and other amenities. A further 3,000 troops could be accommodated in times of "total emergency" (900 were sleeping in the Drill Hall when it suffered a direct hit from two bombs in September 1917, which killed over 130 men).[[144]](#cite_note-144)

The barracks were set to close in 1961 when the majority of naval personnel were withdrawn from Chatham;[[145]](#cite_note-govt-145) however, it went on to serve instead as the RN Supply and Secretariat School in succession to [HMS *Ceres*](/wiki/HMS_Ceres "HMS Ceres"), before finally being closed along with the Dockyard in 1984. The majority of its buildings are still standing, several of them occupied by the [Universities at Medway](/wiki/Universities_at_Medway "Universities at Medway").[[146]](#cite_note-146)
